rationale: I split Business & Management into 14 common, practitioner-facing subspecialties that reflect how academics and industry professionals identify themselves (e.g., Strategy, Marketing, Operations). The count balances breadth (covering functional areas like HR, Accounting, and IS) with depth (separating Entrepreneurship, Corporate Governance, and Analytics). I deliberately kept Finance-related market/asset topics out because those belong to Economics & Finance, and I merged closely related practice areas (Operations & Supply Chain) to avoid overly granular cards. A potentially controversial split was Marketing vs. Consumer Behavior—I kept a single Marketing card to reduce overlap and rely on article tags for fine-grained subtopics instead of separate picker cards.

subspecialties.name: Strategy
subspecialties.tagline: Corporate & competitive strategy, strategic planning, M&A
covers: Firm-level strategy, competitive positioning, business models
Examples: corporate diversification, M&A studies, strategic alliances
explicitly_excludes: corporate-governance — governance, boards, and stewardship work belongs there
entrepreneurship — early-stage startup strategy belongs to Entrepreneurship

subspecialties.name: Marketing
subspecialties.tagline: Branding, advertising, pricing, market research, consumer insights
covers: Marketing strategy, advertising effectiveness, pricing and product policy
Examples: brand equity studies, field experiments on ads, pricing experiments
explicitly_excludes: business-analytics — methodological/ML-heavy market analytics belongs there
consumer-behavior — if behavioral science explicitly framed as psychology, route to Organizational Behavior

subspecialties.name: Organizational Behavior
subspecialties.tagline: Individual and group behavior, teams, leadership, workplace psychology
covers: Micro-level studies of employee behavior, motivation, leadership, teams
Examples: field experiments on incentives, studies of decision-making in organizations
explicitly_excludes: human-resources — HR systems and practices operationalized at policy level belong there
strategy — top-management strategic decision research stays in Strategy

subspecialties.name: Operations & Supply Chain
subspecialties.tagline: Process design, manufacturing, logistics, service operations, SCM
covers: Operations management, inventory, production planning, logistics and supply chains
Examples: queuing/service operations, inventory policy, last-mile logistics
explicitly_excludes: business-analytics — analytics methods for ops may be cross-listed but are in Analytics when methods-focused

subspecialties.name: Entrepreneurship
subspecialties.tagline: New ventures, startups, VC, founding teams, new-product introduction
covers: Founding processes, startup finance (non-market), scaling, incubators
Examples: founder-team dynamics, entrepreneurial ecosystems, seed-stage studies
explicitly-excludes: strategy — established-firm strategic questions belong in Strategy
economics-and-finance — market/asset pricing of startups goes to Economics & Finance

subspecialties.name: Information Systems
subspecialties.tagline: IT strategy, digital platforms, IS adoption, socio-technical systems
covers: Digital platforms, enterprise systems, IT management and governance
Examples: platform competition, IT adoption research, digital transformation
explicitly-excludes: business-analytics — method-heavy ML/AI research on data science models belongs there
innovation-and-technology — product R&D and technology management belongs to Innovation & Technology Management

subspecialties.name: Human Resources
subspecialties.tagline: Workplace policies, talent management, recruitment, compensation systems
covers: HR practices, performance management, staffing systems, labor relations (non-macro)
Examples: hiring algorithms, pay-for-performance programs, training interventions
explicitly-excludes: organizational-behavior — micro-theory/behavioral experiments on individuals or teams belong there
economics-and-finance — labor economics questions go to Economics & Finance

subspecialties.name: Business Analytics & Data Science
subspecialties.tagline: Applied analytics, ML for business, causal inference, experimentation
covers: Applied machine learning, causal methods, A/B testing, forecasting for business
Examples: uplift modeling for marketing, causal inference of management interventions
explicitly-excludes: information-systems — socio-technical IT adoption questions go there
economics-and-finance — novel econometric theory belongs to other categories

subspecialties.name: Accounting
subspecialties.tagline: Financial & managerial accounting, disclosure, audit, reporting
covers: Accounting information, reporting quality, auditing, managerial accounting systems
Examples: earnings management studies, audit market structure, cost accounting
explicitly-excludes: economics-and-finance — asset pricing and market microstructure studies go to Economics & Finance
corporate-governance — board oversight research may be cross-listed but governance card owns that

subspecialties.name: Innovation & Technology Management
subspecialties.tagline: R&D management, product development, technology strategy, diffusion
covers: Innovation processes, R&D organization, technology adoption and diffusion
Examples: open innovation, patent strategy, new product development studies
explicitly-excludes: information-systems — digital platform tech strategy belongs there when IT-centric
strategy — firm-level strategic outcomes remain in Strategy

subspecialties.name: Corporate Governance & Ethics
subspecialties.tagline: Boards, ownership, corporate law interface, CSR, business ethics
covers: Board structure, shareholder relations, executive compensation, CSR and ethics
Examples: board independence effects, stakeholder governance, corporate social responsibility
explicitly-excludes: strategy — strategy papers with governance as a minor control belong in Strategy
law-and-policy — legal doctrine or regulation-heavy work may route to adjacent policy/law categories

subspecialties.name: International Business
subspecialties.tagline: Cross-border strategy, multinational firms, trade, FDI, localization
covers: Multinational enterprise strategy, cross-border management, global supply chains
Examples: FDI strategy, subsidiary management, international joint ventures
explicitly-excludes: economics-and-finance — macro trade and international macroeconomics go to Economics & Finance
strategy — purely domestic strategy research remains in Strategy

subspecialties.name: Project & Program Management
subspecialties.tagline: Project planning, governance, execution, portfolios and PMO practices
covers: Project management methodologies, program governance, agile vs. waterfall evaluations
Examples: IT project failure analyses, large program governance studies
explicitly-excludes: operations-and-supply-chain — process-level operational research stays there
business-analytics — scheduling/optimization models that are method-centric go to Analytics

uncovered_concerns: Finance (financial markets, asset pricing) was intentionally excluded and routed to Economics & Finance — corporate finance topics that are managerial were retained within relevant subspecialties.
Consumer Behavior considered as a separate card but merged into Marketing/Organizational Behavior to avoid duplication and overlap in the picker grid.
Legal/regulatory scholarship and public policy-heavy work can overlap; I kept governance/ethics here but heavyweight legal theory should route to Law/Policy if available.

Very niche applied domains (healthcare management, sports management, hospitality) were not split out as separate cards — these are often tagged within functional cards (Operations, Strategy, HR) or should be handled by subject-matter filters.
